content:
Hi, I've been looking into the R4L project recently, currently diving into its crate build process.

I've noticed the usage of unstable features in the kernel crate, like #![feature(allocator_api)].
My experience tells me that these features are only available in nightly compiler versions.
Additionally, I've observed the use of certain compilation options,
such as -Zbinary_dep_depinfo. I tried to verify it myself and confirmed that the compiler isn't the nightly version but a stable one, as specified by ../scripts/min-tool-version.sh.
Could anyone help explain this to me?
You can read more about the use of unstable features here: https://rust-for-linux.com/unstable-features

Okay, thank you. Let me explain further about my question: when compiling Rust in the kernel, why can we use the '-Z' option and by what method is this achieved? When I use this option, I encounter compilation errors, indicating that only the nightly version can use it."

You are correct in saying that we are using a stable compiler. And stable compilers cannot use unstable features. But there is a backdoor installed in the compiler: RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP=1. You can read more about it here. Essentially it was created to build the next version of rustc using the most up-to-date beta compiler, which also does not allow unstable features. But as you know, the compiler makes heavy use of unstable features as well, so they allow enabling unstable features either when you are running on nightly or when RUSTC_BOOTSTRAP=1.
Thank you very much. I believe it might be able to clarify my doubts. Continuous learning.
